text: Current spatial transcriptomics technologies offer gene expression information at the right place and at the time, which demands efficient analysis for clustering in the spatial context, multi-sample integration and cell type deconvolution. In this work, we proposed a graph self-supervised contrastive learning framework GraphST to fully explore the underlying information of single-cell spatial transcriptomics data. GraphST integrates graph neural networks and self-supervised learning to produce powerful and discriminative features for ST data by minimizing the distance of neighboring points in the embedding space in the spatial context. Through experiments on various types of tissues and ST technologies, we showed that GraphST is generally better than state-of-the-art methods. In terms of local analysis, GraphST achieves a 10% improvement in clustering accuracy and reveals finer anatomical details in mouse brain and embryo samples. As for multi-sample analysis, GraphST supports both vertical and horizontal integration and can effectively remove batch effects. GraphST also has a better performance in cell type deconvolution, which can capture the intricate spatial niches such as germinal centers in lymph nodes and exhausted tumor-infiltrating T cells in breast cancer.
headline: Fully Human Written
